message:
feat(rchd): add disk_pressure module for worker storage pressure monitoring and policy evaluation

Introduce a new `disk_pressure` module in `rchd` that provides daemon-side
disk-pressure monitoring and policy classification for remote workers. This
is the foundation for preventing mid-build failures caused by workers running
out of disk space (bd-vvmd.4.2).

Why this exists:
Remote compilation builds can fail mid-flight when a worker's disk fills up
during artifact generation. Rather than reacting to failures after they occur,
the daemon now proactively monitors worker storage state and classifies each
worker into a normalized pressure band (Healthy/Warning/Critical/TelemetryGap)
that downstream scheduling and status surfaces can consume.

Key design decisions and details:

- PressureState enum (Healthy, Warning, Critical, TelemetryGap) provides stable,
  low-cardinality scheduling signals suitable for automation consumers.

- PressureConfidence enum (High, Medium, Low) communicates decision quality so
  that downstream consumers can apply appropriate caution (e.g., fail-open when
  confidence is Low due to missing telemetry).

- PressureAssessment struct carries the full policy decision payload including
  measured evidence (disk_free_gb, disk_total_gb, disk_free_ratio, disk_io_util_pct,
  memory_pressure), telemetry freshness, and provenance (reason_code, policy_rule).

- DiskPressurePolicyConfig provides tunable thresholds for warning/critical bands
  across disk space (absolute GB and ratio), I/O saturation, and memory pressure,
  with configurable poll cadence and telemetry staleness window.

- DiskPressureMonitor runs as a background tokio task, periodically evaluating all
  workers against the policy config and storing assessments on WorkerState for
  scheduler consumption.

- Policy evaluation distinguishes fresh-telemetry and stale-telemetry paths:
  with fresh telemetry, all signals (disk, I/O, memory) contribute to classification;
  without fresh telemetry, only capability-reported disk metrics are used with
  reduced confidence, preserving fail-open behavior.

- Compound rules (e.g., high I/O saturation combined with low headroom) trigger
  Critical classification to catch scenarios where individual metrics alone might
  only reach Warning.

- Structured tracing logs emit full policy decision context (worker, state,
  confidence, reason_code, policy_rule, all measured values) at appropriate
  severity levels (warn for Critical, info for Warning/TelemetryGap, debug for
  Healthy), enabling operator diagnostics and post-incident analysis.

- Comprehensive unit tests cover all classification paths: healthy with fresh
  metrics, warning thresholds, critical thresholds, stale telemetry degradation,
  critical-even-with-stale-telemetry, and missing disk metrics.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
